1.note: My go-to for Korean BBQ, right by Gongdeok Station where the whole area smells of grilling meat. We made sure Anthony Bourdain ate here.

2.note: Packed, packed, packed. They pre-cook your meat over strong fire, then finish it at your table. Catch it before gentrification wins.

3.note: You can't go wrong with Two-Two. A franchise that acts like a mom-and-pop, run by a welcoming couple outside Gongdeok Station. I want them to get more love.

4.note: No fried here. Chicken is spit-roasted over wood and served on sizzling platters of crispy rice. The ones near Gongdeok and Sookmyung kill.

5.note: Classic-style chicken since 1986, brined 24 hours then coated in spicy powder and fried. Go mild, or take on the spicy version as a satisfying challenge.

6.note: Get the chicken three ways: fried, sauced, and garlic. Then the pa dalk, boneless thighs over ribbons of green onion. My favorite spot is run by two ladies.

7.note: Past the buskers is what I call Crazy Street Food Alley: rotating stalls throwing every new street food idea against the wall to see what sticks.

8.note: Where real Koreans shop and young vendors reinvent street food: deep-fried bulgogi dumplings, creme brulee hotteok, and next-gen bungeoppang.

9.note: Yeah, it's just bossam, but consistently good and best in the restaurant itself. The banchan is always diverse, including that sweet, fruity fresh kimchi.

10.note: A guy quit his job and spent months perfecting the recipe. The result is spicy, addictive tteokbokki. Mix it with the soondae, odeng, and crispy twigim.

11.note: Big Hit Chicken, the old standby family-style joint. Reliable, predictable, but satisfying.

12.note: The franchise that made me wonder who orders bingsu in winter. Turns out plenty of people. They specialize in Instagrammy shaved ice, and that's a good thing.

13.note: Tiny, tiny, tiny place near Gyeongbokgung with just two items: kimchi jjigae and gyeran mari. Get both.

14.note: Respect, respect, respect. Grandmother has made good bibimbap here for over 50 years. Simple menu: get the bibimbap and the kongnamul gukbap.

15.note: A favorite of former president Roh Mu-hyun, in a classic hanok complex serving one thing: samgyetang. I'm not a fan of it, but I make an exception here.

16.note: Explore Gwangjang and try some bindaeddeok, one of my favorites, washed down with some road beers. That's always fun.

17.note: Not a soulless theme park but a tight-knit warren of century-old hanok filled with cocktail dens, handmade crafts, and surprisingly good bistros.

18.note: Book a temple food tasting near Jogyesa. It's vegan, but don't panic: Korean Buddhist food means deep flavors, fermented everything, and zero fake-meat nonsense.

19.note: Home of Insa-dong hotteok, with cornmeal in the dough for extra crunch. Also fire-grilled chicken on a stick and my beloved egg breads.

20.note: In the alley maze south of Jongno 3-ga, a joint serving bossam with oysters for over 35 years. Weekend lines out the door, and it's good!

21.note: My new favorite franchise, all about housemade tofu made right in the window. Get the Dubu Bossam set and the crunchy Dubu Jeon. Pure protein.

22.note: Industrial workshops bathed in green and pink neon. Tiny staircases lead to smoky hideouts where bartenders serve cocktails in teacups.

23.note: Famous, almost too famous. A bit stodgy, the place you take grandma after church, but the naengmyeon is worth the premium price.

24.note: The owner cooked for North Korean army officers before defecting. Now his cozy shop serves the dishes he misses: eat the naengmyeon and the soondae.

25.note: A 1970s-nostalgia chain popular with Koreans and expats. Order the yeoltan bulgogi, shaved pork in spicy sauce, and grill it. Look for the yellow roof.

26.note: Oven chicken territory. Goobne bills itself as a healthy alternative to fried, and when we order it, it's stripped clean to the bone.

27.note: Street food for the tourist crowd, and where the weirder inventions debut. The Tornado Potato was born right here.

28.note: A franchise, but one of the better ones. Jjimdalk is braised garlicky sweet chicken, rich and hearty. Come with company for these huge plates.

29.note: My Myeongdong lunch spot. Chef Eddie Ahn trained in Italy, so no sugar-coated garlic bread here: by far the best Italian in Myeong-dong.

30.note: The Chinese embassy is nearby, so the Chinese food is good. This hot pot franchise is English-friendly with a sauce bar. Go for the spicy Sichuan broth.

31.note: Alabama native Linus Kim went to America to learn from the best, then opened here. Slow-cooked, perfectly seasoned Q so good it doesn't need the sauce.

32.note: Great stuff! Dark-meat chicken in sweet-spicy sauce, grilled on charcoal in front of you. Reasonably priced: one order feeds two, or one Joe.

33.note: I love Gogung. The Jeonju original got hard to find in Seoul, but this Starfield Mall branch serves classic royal Jeonju bibimbap in brass bowls.

34.note: Head to Jamsil, across the lake from Lotte World, up to the second floor. This flagship serves 3-year-old kimchi jjigae, surprisingly subtle and smooth.

35.note: The one Americans picture for Korean fried chicken: batter-dipped, garlicky, shatteringly crisp. Order it yangnyeom, but beware the hot geyser of juice on first bite.

36.note: Nureungji tongdalk, scorched-rice fried chicken from Gangwon. Know it by the rotisserie birds, oak logs out front, and heavenly smoke. Your new chicken-and-beer pairing.

37.note: A brand of many Korean restaurants, all good. The Clay Pot Duck, stuffed with rice, seeds, and medicinal ingredients and baked for hours, is Thanksgiving in Korea.

38.note: Seoul's largest meat market, but tourists rarely go because it smells like beef. Pick your Hanwoo, grill it upstairs with the guys who butchered it. Glorious.
38.tip: Many butchers throw in complimentary service cuts, often lean beef to eat raw with sesame oil and salt.

39.note: Once a grimy shoe-factory district, now where Seoul's creative class sips espresso in concrete bunkers and shops indie pop-ups inside shipping containers.

40.note: One of the best places in Seoul: an aquarium where you eat the exhibits. Much of the fish is still alive in tanks, so it barely smells.

41.note: My favorite part of the market: all this fermented salted seafood, toothpicks out for sampling. My guilty pleasure is the spicy raw oysters on buttery crackers.

42.note: A favorite secret corner: a row of streetside vendors feeding students cramming for exams. Cheap, varied, good. Hunt down the super-spicy Bomb Rice.

43.note: Watch for the lunch lines. The naengmyeon is great, though it helps that it's one of the few good restaurants in Yeouido.

44.note: I recommend department store basements for food journeys and foodie souvenirs. The stalls constantly change, so just go check it out and sample anything interesting.

45.name: Dragon Hill Spa 용산드래곤힐스파
45.note: Hit a real jjimjilbang. Sweat in a kiln, nap on a heated floor, snack on baked eggs and cold sikhye. You'll emerge cleaner, softer, and slightly dehydrated.

46.note: Naengmyeon always hits after a barbecue meal, with a few squirts of vinegar and hot mustard. That's why places like Woo Lae Oak are famous for it.

47.note: Coffee and coal vibes, with outlets and moody lighting to work from. Just remember: camp for six hours and you'd better buy a second drink.

48.note: One of the great charms of Korea: grab drinks, cups, and snacks, pull up a table outside a convenience store, and you've got a cheap all-night spot.

49.note: You'll never forget this makgeolli bar, pouring regional brews in many flavors. The fusion is the good kind: get the American Potato Pancake with sour cream and bacon.

50.note: Wear a hanbok for free entry, arrive early before the tour buses, and actually take in the architecture. Then hit the National Folk Museum behind it.
50.tip: Skip the Changing of the Guard if you're short on time: it's more cosplay than ceremony.

51.name: Inwangsan Hike 인왕산
51.note: Forget Namsan Tower. This hike has better views, fewer tourists, and no overpriced elevator tickets. Plus you might pass a shrine or shamanic altar along the way.
51.tip: Best for hikers and photographers; skip it on rainy days unless you enjoy slipping on wet pine needles.

52.note: I get tailor-made shirts here. They keep my specs on file, so I just pick fabric and style and they ship within ten days, even overseas.

53.note: Skip the Myeongdong crowds and head east to Dongmyo: part thrift paradise, part museum of random antiques, vintage leather jackets beside dusty LPs. It still feels real.

54.note: Six hundred traditional hanok on a hillside between two palaces, still residential. Bukchon Alley 11 gives the most photographed view in the city: tiled rooftops against the skyline.
54.tip: Come before 09:00 or after 17:00; the signs asking for quiet are not decorative.

55.note: The most intact of Seoul's five royal palaces and UNESCO-listed. The Huwon (Secret Garden) at the rear is the real reason to come, accessible by timed tour only.
55.tip: Book the Huwon tour online weeks ahead; it sells out fast in spring and autumn.

56.name: Samcheong-dong 삼청동
56.hours: Most galleries and shops Tue-Sun 10:00-18:00; closed Mon.
56.note: The street from Gyeongbokgung to Bukchon has Seoul's highest density of independent galleries, around thirty in converted hanok. The craft shops sell celadon and hanji well above Insadong quality.
56.tip: Walk Samcheong-ro from Anguk Station toward Bukchon; the galleries cluster densest near Bukchon-ro.

57.note: A six-kilometre stream through central Seoul, restored in 2005 after they tore down the highway that had buried it for four decades. Now a linear park below the city grid.

58.note: Zaha Hadid's largest project: 86,000 square metres of aluminium curves with no straight walls, opened in 2014. Best at night when the surface is lit.

59.note: Asia's largest museum by collection, and free. Korean history from the Palaeolithic to Joseon: gold crowns, the world's finest Goryeo celadon, a 14-metre indoor pagoda.
59.tip: Allow three hours minimum; the cafe terrace faces Namsan Tower across the reflecting pool, a peaceful lunch stop.

60.note: Samsung's private museum, built by Botta, Nouvel, and Koolhaas. Historic Korean celadon and bronze meet contemporary works by Lee Ufan, Hirst, and Kapoor. One of Asia's strongest.
60.tip: Book online, weekends sell out; the shop has Seoul's best art books and design objects.

61.name: Garosu-gil 가로수길
61.note: A 700-metre ginkgo-lined boulevard of independent boutiques, beauty flagships, and specialty coffee, best in autumn. The most interesting spots have retreated into the side alleys behind.
61.tip: Skip the main strip on weekends; the side alleys hold the independent boutiques and quieter cafes.

62.note: The neighbourhood west of Hongdae, now Seoul's densest strip of independent cafes, natural wine bars, and single-item restaurants, still without the tourists.
62.tip: Go mid-week; the natural wine bars on the west end of Yeonnam-ro are best after 20:00.

63.note: Twelve riverside parks line the Han; Yeouido is the most used. Summer evenings the banks fill with Seoulites picnicking on delivery food, local culture most visitors miss entirely.
63.tip: Go at dusk; order fried chicken delivery to the entrance, exactly how Seoulites eat on summer nights.

64.note: A granite massif rising to 836 metres on Seoul's edge, 30 minutes by metro. The Baegundae route ends on bare granite with an unobstructed panorama of the city.
64.tip: Go on a weekday and bring water; there are no vendors above the visitor centre.

65.name: Gwanghwamun Square 광화문광장
65.note: Redesigned in 2022, doubled in width with traffic removed, a proper civic forecourt to Gyeongbokgung. The Yi Sun-sin and King Sejong statues stand on axis with the palace gate.
65.tip: On clear days the view frames Baegundae peak in Bukhansan directly behind the palace gate.
